name: lentil wellington
instructions:
From Nisha Vora (Rainbow Plant Life). A vegan wellington built on a lentil-and-mushroom log filling wrapped in puff pastry — no vegan filets, no seared protein slabs. The whole roast is one continuous savory-baked log, umami-forward from mushrooms + walnuts + miso + nutritional yeast + tomato paste. Called 'lentil wellington' here to distinguish it from the vegan-wellington recipe in this cookbook (which uses vegan filets in the classical individual-portion beef-wellington format — see sibling note).

Serves 8. Total time ~2.5 hours (20 min prep, 2 hr 10 min cook + bake).

=== FILLING INGREDIENTS ===
- ~1/3 cup (60g) dried brown or green lentils (~6 oz cooked) — or use pre-cooked lentils as a shortcut
- 1 tbsp olive oil
- 1 small yellow onion, chopped
- 6 garlic cloves, chopped
- 8 oz (~227g) mushrooms — Nisha uses a mix of shiitake and cremini; any mushroom works (see notes)
- 1 tbsp fresh thyme leaves (or 1 tsp dried)
- 1 tbsp fresh rosemary leaves, chopped (or 1 tsp dried)
- 1 tsp sweet or hot paprika (optional — adds reddish color)
- freshly cracked black pepper
- 1 tsp kosher salt, plus more to taste
- 1 cup (~110g) walnuts, toasted (see notes — don't skip toasting)
- 2 tbsp white or yellow (light) miso paste
- 2 tbsp nutritional yeast
- 1 tbsp tomato paste
- 1 tbsp fresh lemon juice
- 1/4 cup (~20g) panko breadcrumbs
- 1/4 cup (32g) all-purpose flour

=== PASTRY ===
- 1 sheet vegan puff pastry (Pepperidge Farms is naturally vegan; check label)
- flour for rolling
- 1 tbsp unsweetened plant-based milk
- 1 tbsp olive oil or neutral oil
- coarse/flaky sea salt for topping

=== PROCEDURE ===

1. COOK THE LENTILS: instant pot — 2/3 cup water + pinch salt, pressure cook high 6 min, natural release 10 min (repeat 1-2 min if not tender). Stovetop — cover lentils with water, boil then simmer covered 20-30 min until tender-not-mushy. Drain any excess. Transfer to a bowl and REFRIGERATE until fully cooled.

2. FINELY CHOP THE MUSHROOMS: pulse in a food processor several times until finely chopped, or knife-chop fine.

3. COOK THE MUSHROOM MIXTURE: heat olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high. Add onions + small pinch of salt; cook 7-10 min stirring occasionally until softened and golden brown. Add garlic, mushrooms, thyme, rosemary, paprika, black pepper; cook ~5 min until mushrooms are softened and their liquid has evaporated. Add 1 tsp salt and cook 1 more min. Transfer to fridge to cool completely.

4. TOAST + GRIND THE WALNUTS: skillet on medium-low 3-5 min tossing frequently, OR oven at 350°F 8-10 min. Cool slightly. Pulse in food processor to fine crumbs — DON'T over-process into walnut butter.

5. PREHEAT OVEN TO 375°F (190°C).

6. MAKE THE FILLING: add cooled lentils + cooled mushroom mixture to the food processor, plus miso, nutritional yeast, tomato paste, and lemon juice. Blend until somewhat smooth, slightly sticky, and coming together. Add the ground walnuts, panko, and flour; blend again until well incorporated. If the filling is warm, refrigerate 15-30 min to make it moldable.

7. FORM + BAKE THE LOG: line a rimmed sheet pan with parchment. Turn out the filling and hand-form into a log — roughly 7 in long x 4.5-5 in wide (18 cm x 11.5-12.5 cm), no taller than 1 in. Bake at 375°F for 35 min until firm to the touch and slightly brown/crisp outside (internal temp 200-205°F / 94-96°C). Cool COMPLETELY. Turn oven off.

8. THAW THE PUFF PASTRY: while log cools, lay puff pastry on a lightly floured surface. Thaw at room temp 20-30 min (no longer — see notes on keeping cold).

9. ROLL + BRAID: roll pastry to ~9 x 13 inches. Place cooled log in the middle so the log's top and bottom nearly touch the pastry edges widthwise. With a paring knife, cut 1-inch strips on each side about 1/4 of the way in (not quite reaching the log) — ~9 strips per side. Whisk plant milk + oil = vegan egg wash. Brush each strip with egg wash. Braid alternating strips over the top of the log. Pinch and tuck the top/bottom edges closed; brush with more egg wash to seal.

10. VENT + REST: lightly prick the top of the pastry all over with a fork (steam venting). Transfer wellington to a fresh parchment-lined sheet pan. Brush the rest of the egg wash all over; sprinkle flaky sea salt on top. Refrigerate 15-20 min to re-chill the pastry (critical — see two-temperature note).

11. TWO-TEMPERATURE BAKE: preheat oven to 375°F (190°C). Bake 25 min. Increase to 425°F (218°C) and bake 10 more min until golden brown, flaky, puffed, and internal temp reads 200-205°F. See notes on why the two-temp system matters.

12. REST + SLICE: cool 10 min before slicing. Serve plain or with mushroom gravy (see gravy component below).

=== OPTIONAL — MUSHROOM GRAVY (SEPARATE, SERVE ALONGSIDE) ===

Nisha frames the gravy as optional ('the loaf is rich and moist enough to do without'). Same ingredient overlap with the filling — batch-prep alongside.

INGREDIENTS:
- 1 tbsp olive oil
- 1 small yellow onion, diced
- 3 garlic cloves, minced
- 1 tbsp fresh thyme leaves (or 1 tsp dried)
- 1 tbsp fresh rosemary leaves, chopped
- 2 cups (~150-200g) chopped cremini (or other brown-button) mushrooms
- black pepper to taste
- 1/2 tsp kosher salt
- 3 tbsp arrowroot powder or cornstarch
- 1 1/2 cups (360 mL) low-sodium vegetable stock
- 1 tbsp white or yellow (light) miso paste

PROCEDURE:
1. Heat olive oil in a large frying pan over medium-high. Add onions and cook ~4 min until softening. Add garlic, mushrooms, thyme, rosemary, black pepper; cook until mushrooms reduce and liquid mostly (not fully) evaporates. Add 1/2 tsp salt; cook 1 more min.
2. Add arrowroot/cornstarch, whisk to coat, cook 1 min. Pour in vegetable stock; bring to a simmer, whisk 2 min until thickened.
3. Transfer to a blender, add miso, blend smooth. Return to pan, low heat 3-5 min to thicken further. Adjust seasoning.

Gravy keeps 4-5 days in the fridge, 1 month in freezer.
notes:
=== NAMED 'LENTIL WELLINGTON' TO DISTINGUISH FROM THE OTHER VEGAN WELLINGTON IN THIS COOKBOOK ===

This cookbook already has a vegan-wellington recipe (possumfodder / Substack — six-component plate with individually seared vegan filets in puff pastry, mashed potatoes, mushroom gravy, mushroom duxelles, and pan-roasted carrots — classical individual-portion beef-wellington format). This recipe (Nisha Vora / Rainbow Plant Life) is structurally different — a single continuous lentil-mushroom log wrapped in puff pastry, more like a nut-loaf-inside-pastry. Both are valid vegan wellington approaches; calling this one 'lentil wellington' makes the distinction sharp.

=== FILLING STRUCTURE — WHY THIS ISN'T JUST A NUT LOAF ===

The filling reads unmistakably meaty despite being fully plant-based. The umami stack is the reason: mushrooms (glutamate) + walnuts (chewy meaty texture + oil) + miso (fermented deep umami) + nutritional yeast (savory cheese-adjacent) + tomato paste (concentrated sweet-savory) all in the same mix. Skimping on any one drops the meatiness noticeably — this isn't a place to trim ingredients.

The lentils are the STRUCTURE (they're what holds the log together into sliceable form), but the flavor is doing the work of the mushroom-walnut-miso-nutritional-yeast-tomato-paste stack. Reading the ingredient list, it's easy to dismiss the lentils; that's a mistake — without them the filling has no bind and won't hold a log shape.

=== TOAST THE WALNUTS — NON-NEGOTIABLE ===

Toasting the walnuts before grinding is what unlocks their meaty roasty depth. Raw walnuts in the filling read as raw walnut — flat and slightly astringent. Toasted walnuts contribute a background campfire savoriness that ties the whole loaf together. 3-5 min in a skillet on medium-low tossing frequently, OR 8-10 min at 350°F in the oven. Watch closely; walnuts burn fast once they hit the sweet spot.

DON'T over-process the toasted walnuts either. Pulse just to fine crumbs — beyond that they release oil and start to turn into walnut butter, which changes the filling texture from meaty-chunky to greasy-smooth.

=== COOL EVERYTHING BEFORE BLENDING ===

Both the lentils AND the sautéed mushroom mixture need to be fully cool before they meet the food processor. Warm ingredients turn the filling too sticky to shape — you'll get a wet paste that won't hold a log. Fridge time is real cook time here; plan for it. If you're in a hurry, spread the cooked components on a sheet pan (thin layer = fast cooling) instead of leaving them in a bowl.

=== KEEP THE PASTRY COLD ===

Puff pastry works because of thin butter/fat layers between dough layers — those layers steam-puff during baking. If the pastry warms up too much (over 30 min at room temp, or from being worked with warm hands), the fat softens and integrates into the dough, and the pastry won't puff — it'll just bake flat.

- Thaw the pastry no more than 30 min at room temp (Nisha's cap). If you need longer, thaw in the fridge.
- After assembly (which puts warm hands on the pastry), rest the assembled wellington in the fridge 15-20 min BEFORE it goes into the oven. This isn't optional — Nisha specifically notes that skipping this step causes uneven baking.
- Don't have a rolling pin? Nisha suggests a chilled wine bottle. Same principle: cold surface, minimal warming.

=== THE TWO-TEMPERATURE BAKE — LOAD-BEARING ===

375°F for 25 min, then 425°F for 10 min. Not one temperature the whole way.

Nisha tested both single-temp approaches and both failed:
- ALL AT 425°F: top browns too fast; inner dough doesn't fully bake through.
- ALL AT 375°F: dough bakes through but pastry isn't beautifully golden.
- SOLUTION: start at 375°F to let the inner dough cook through gently, then finish at 425°F to blast the exterior into golden-puffed territory.

This is a real technique note — don't hedge to 400°F the whole time. The two-temperature system is what gives you both structural doneness AND the finished exterior color.

=== FLAKY SALT ON TOP IS NOT OPTIONAL ===

Coarse or flaky sea salt sprinkled on the pastry before baking is one of those low-effort finishes that changes the whole eating experience. The salt crystals crunch against the flaky pastry and contrast with the softer savory filling underneath. Regular table salt doesn't do this — the crystal structure has to be big enough to survive the bake. Maldon or similar flaky sea salt is what you want.

=== MUSHROOM CHOICE — MIX IS BETTER THAN ONE VARIETY ===

Nisha uses a mix of shiitake and cremini. Any mushroom works but a mix reads more interesting than one variety alone — different mushroom species have different flavor profiles and moisture contents, and the mix gives you a more complex background.

- SHIITAKE: earthier, more umami-forward, denser.
- CREMINI (brown button): milder, sweeter, more familiar 'mushroom' flavor.
- WHITE BUTTON: higher water content — cook longer to drive off moisture, or the filling stays wet.
- PORTABELLA: works, deeper meatier flavor; would tilt the loaf more assertively mushroomy.

All are valid. The shiitake + cremini mix Nisha uses is a solid default.

=== MAKE-AHEAD OPTIONS ===

Nisha provides two make-ahead paths:
1. FILLING AHEAD: make the mushroom-lentil log through step 7 (bake and cool), refrigerate up to 2 days. On serving day, wrap in pastry and bake per steps 8-12.
2. FULLY-ASSEMBLED-UNBAKED AHEAD: assemble the wellington through step 10, wrap tightly and refrigerate up to 1 day. Bake as directed on serving day.

The filling-ahead option is the better one for planning — the pastry-wrap-and-bake step on serving day is the shortest part of the recipe.

=== GRAVY IS OPTIONAL (NISHA'S OWN NOTE) ===

Nisha explicitly says 'personally, I think this loaf is rich and moist enough to do without the gravy.' The filling has enough umami and moisture on its own. Make the gravy if serving to guests who expect wellington-with-gravy or for a fancier presentation; skip it for a simpler execution. The ingredient overlap with the filling means batch-prep is efficient if you want it.

=== MISO + NUTRITIONAL YEAST HERE IS A LEGITIMATE WIN — DESPITE THE USUAL CRITICISM ===

The miso + nutritional yeast combination is the classic 'vegan glutamate bomb' — a shortcut umami stack that most vegan recipes over-use to compensate for missing animal fat and gelatin. I'm generally critical of it: it can read one-note savory and hide behind processed savoriness rather than building real flavor from technique.

BUT IT WORKS HERE. In this recipe the miso + nutritional yeast sits inside a broader flavor architecture — toasted walnuts (roasty), mushrooms (glutamate + texture), tomato paste (sweet + tangy + concentrated), thyme + rosemary (herbal woodsy), lemon juice (bright), sautéed onion + 6 garlic cloves (structural aromatics). The miso and nutritional yeast add depth to that stack rather than substituting for it. The loaf reads meaty and multidimensional, not glutamate-forward.

Why it lands here specifically: the wellington format wants a filling that reads meaty enough to stand alongside classical beef-wellington expectations. The miso + nutritional yeast is doing the specific job of pushing the umami into meat-analog territory that other umami sources (mushroom, walnut, tomato paste) can't reach on their own. This is the kind of application where the vegan-glutamate-bomb combination is genuinely correct — not a shortcut, a structural tool.

General rule I'm updating for myself: miso + nutritional yeast is bad when it's the WHOLE umami stack; it's good when it's part of a broader one.

=== ANGLICAN VARIATION (WHAT I ACTUALLY MADE) ===

The first time I made this I departed from Nisha's ingredient list in a few places, and the result read more distinctly British / anglican than Nisha's original — pushing the dish closer to its beef-wellington heritage. Worth capturing as a real variation:

- FAT: butter + vegetable oil instead of olive oil. Butter is the classical english fat and it reads more traditionally-british than olive oil (which pulls the dish mediterranean). Vegetable oil cuts the butter for smoke point during the mushroom sauté.
- DEPTH: worcestershire sauce and/or tamarind paste instead of tomato paste. Worcestershire is the anglican umami condiment (tamarind + anchovy + molasses + spices) and it slots into the same depth-and-tang slot tomato paste fills, but in a distinctly english register. Tamarind paste alone also works. Either or both.
- HERBS: kept the thyme, swapped rosemary for dill. Thyme + dill reads more traditionally-english (dill is at home in british / northern-european cooking; rosemary is more mediterranean / italian). Rosemary + thyme is fine and mediterranean-italian in register; thyme + dill pulls the dish anglican.
- ONION: onion powder instead of diced fresh onion. Either works — the fresh onion adds sweetness and moisture that the powder doesn't, but the powder is quicker and reads more even in the finished loaf. Fresh is probably slightly better; both are valid.

Cumulative effect: more distinctly british than Nisha's original. Better or worse depends on what you want — Nisha's version reads more contemporary-vegan; my anglican version reads more like the classical beef wellington it's descended from.

=== VARIATIONS FROM THE COMMENTS SECTION (WORTH KNOWING) ===

- COMMENTER 'ANN': subbed eggplant for mushrooms, doubled seasonings. Reported success. Interesting variation to try if mushrooms aren't around.
- COMMENTER 'DAKSHA': accidentally over-processed mushrooms to a very fine ground-beef-like texture; reported it turned out well anyway. Suggests the mushroom-chop coarseness has some flexibility — not a place to worry about being exact.

=== SIBLING RECIPES ===

- vegan-wellington: the possumfodder six-component plate with individual seared vegan filets. Structurally distinct — this one is a single log, that one is individually portioned filets. Both are 'vegan wellington'; pick based on whether you want a communal roast (this) or plated individual portions (that).
- lentil-mushroom-stew: same lentil + mushroom umami DNA, but in stew form. If you like the flavor of this loaf but don't want to bother with the pastry, that's the shortcut.
- coq-au-vin, rosemary-chicken-pot-pie, french-chicken-soup: the french poultry mains this would sit alongside on a holiday table. Different food, similar occasion.
